resume:
NADIA OKAFOR
Master of Public Health candidate (Epidemiology track), expected 2026 — Hartwell University School of Public Health
B.S. Biology, minor in Health Informatics, 2024 — Hartwell University | GPA 3.74
Target role: Clinical Quality Improvement Intern (hospital operations)

SUMMARY
MPH candidate with applied experience in clinical quality improvement, infection-prevention surveillance, and healthcare data analysis. Comfortable working at the intersection of frontline clinical workflows and data, translating chart-level detail into measurable process change while respecting patient privacy and regulatory constraints. Seeking a hospital-based quality-improvement internship where I can contribute to measurable reductions in preventable harm.

EDUCATION
Master of Public Health, Epidemiology — Hartwell University (in progress)
- Relevant coursework: Healthcare Quality & Safety, Biostatistics I-II, Epidemiologic Methods, Health Systems & Policy, Applied Regression, Survey of Electronic Health Records.
- Capstone (in progress): a retrospective analysis of catheter-associated urinary tract infection (CAUTI) rates across three medical-surgical units.

Bachelor of Science, Biology (minor: Health Informatics) — Hartwell University
- Honors thesis on antimicrobial-stewardship adherence in an outpatient setting.
- Dean's List four semesters.

EXPERIENCE

Quality Improvement Intern — Riverbend Regional Medical Center (Summer 2024, 10 weeks)
- Embedded with the Infection Prevention and Quality department, supporting the medical-surgical service line's effort to reduce central-line-associated bloodstream infections (CLABSI).
- Conducted structured chart audits on central-line maintenance bundle compliance, reviewing documentation for dressing changes, hub disinfection ('scrub the hub'), and daily line-necessity assessment.
- Built a simple run chart in Excel tracking weekly bundle-compliance percentage against CLABSI events, which the unit's nurse manager began using in the weekly safety huddle.
- Shadowed the bedside nursing workflow to understand where documentation broke down versus where the actual practice broke down — and learned these are not the same problem.
- Drafted a one-page standardized audit checklist that reduced the time per chart audit, later adopted by two other auditors on the team.
- Presented preliminary findings at the department's monthly quality meeting, focusing on a single high-yield gap: missing daily line-necessity documentation on the night shift.
- Cross-walked the unit's audit findings against the CDC NHSN definitions for CLABSI to make sure the team was counting events consistently with how they would be reported externally.
- Interviewed three night-shift nurses informally to understand the workflow constraints behind the documentation gap, surfacing that the necessity field was buried several clicks deep in the EHR and easy to skip during a busy shift.
- Maintained a de-identified audit log so that no patient identifiers left the secure environment, and double-checked each export against the minimum-necessary standard before sharing summaries.

Research Assistant — Hartwell Health Data Lab (2023 - present)
- Support a faculty-led project linking electronic health record data to readmission outcomes for heart-failure patients.
- Clean and de-identify structured EHR extracts in accordance with HIPAA Safe Harbor de-identification standards.
- Wrote reusable data-cleaning scripts in R that standardized medication-name fields across two source systems.
- Produced descriptive tables and figures for a manuscript currently under internal review.
- Documented the data dictionary and the de-identification steps so the pipeline is reproducible by future research assistants.
- Flagged a data-quality issue where two encounters were duplicated across source systems, and proposed a deduplication rule keyed on encounter timestamp and medical-record number hash.

Volunteer — Community Free Clinic (2022 - 2024)
- Served as an intake volunteer for an uninsured-patient clinic two evenings per month.
- Collected vitals, recorded chief complaints, and managed the paper-to-digital intake handoff.
- Helped pilot a no-show reminder process for follow-up appointments, and tracked whether reminded patients returned at a higher rate than the prior baseline.
- Learned to navigate language and literacy barriers during intake, often coordinating with a volunteer interpreter to make sure chief complaints were recorded accurately.

Certified Nursing Assistant (CNA) — Maplewood Skilled Nursing (2021 - 2022, part-time)
- Provided direct resident care: activities of daily living, vitals, and accurate documentation in the facility EHR.
- Recognized for documentation accuracy during a state survey readiness audit.

TECHNICAL & DOMAIN SKILLS
- Data & analysis: R (data cleaning, descriptive stats, ggplot2), Excel (run charts, pivot tables), basic SQL, REDCap for data capture.
- Clinical & QI methods: chart abstraction, PDSA cycles, run-chart and control-chart interpretation, root-cause analysis (fishbone, 5 Whys), care-bundle compliance auditing.
- Regulatory & safety: HIPAA Safe Harbor de-identification, basics of CMS quality measures (CLABSI, CAUTI, readmissions), IRB submission process (assisted).
- Communication: presenting QI findings to clinical staff, building one-page visual summaries for huddles.

CERTIFICATIONS & TRAINING
- Certified Nursing Assistant (state-licensed, active).
- CITI Program: Human Subjects Research and HIPAA modules completed.
- Basic Life Support (BLS) certified.
- IHI Open School: foundational quality and safety certificate.

LEADERSHIP & ACTIVITIES
- Vice President, Public Health Student Association — coordinated a campus vaccination-awareness drive.
- Peer mentor for first-generation MPH students.
- Volunteer data analyst for a local mutual-aid organization's food-access survey.

HONORS
- Graduate Merit Scholarship, Hartwell School of Public Health.
- Undergraduate Biology Department research award.

REFERENCES
Available on request, including my preceptor from the Riverbend QI internship and my faculty research advisor.
jd: Riverbend Regional Medical Center is hiring a Clinical Quality Improvement Intern for our Patient Safety & Infection Prevention department (returning interns encouraged to apply). The intern supports active improvement work on hospital-acquired-infection bundles, including chart abstraction, run-chart maintenance, and synthesizing findings for frontline clinical teams. You will work alongside infection preventionists, nurse managers, and a quality director, and may present to a unit-based safety committee. We screen for precision and intellectual honesty: when we ask about a result you contributed to, we want exact, scoped answers — what was measured, over what period, what your specific role was, and where the data is uncertain. Vague or inflated claims are disqualifying in a patient-safety context. Requirements: coursework or experience in public health, nursing, or a health-related field; familiarity with quality-improvement methods (PDSA, run charts); strict adherence to patient-privacy rules; and the ability to communicate clearly with clinical staff. Prior QI or clinical exposure is preferred.

history.q: Welcome back to Riverbend. Last summer you supported the CLABSI reduction work. What exactly did you measure, and over what time window?
You built a run chart of weekly bundle compliance against CLABSI events. What was the compliance baseline when you started, and did you see it move during your ten weeks?
You flagged missing daily line-necessity documentation on nights. How confident are you that this was a documentation gap versus an actual practice gap — and how would you tell the difference?
If you came back this summer, what's the one change you'd propose first to close that night-shift gap, and how would you know if it worked?

candidate_last_answer: I'd propose a single change first: add daily line-necessity to the night-shift handoff checklist as a hard stop, so the oncoming nurse can't close handoff without recording it. I'd scope it to the two med-surg units I audited, not the whole hospital. To know if it worked, I'd track two things over eight weeks: documentation completeness for the necessity assessment, which I expect to rise quickly because it's a charting fix, and the actual rate of lines still in place past day five, which is the real clinical signal and would move slower if at all. I'd be careful not to claim a CLABSI reduction from this alone — my sample is far too small to attribute infection-rate changes to one intervention, so I'd treat CLABSI as a balancing measure I watch, not a primary endpoint I take credit for.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: I'd treat CLABSI as a balancing measure I watch, not a primary endpoint I take credit for

expected-yield: the candidate's reasoning about a real risk of the hard-stop change (alert fatigue, false charting to clear the stop, or night-shift workflow burden) and how she would detect that unintended consequence — since the answer is already metric-precise, the next move is to probe judgment on second-order effects
